Suppose that $f(z)$ is a continuous complex-valued function on a clos $S_{r_{0}}\left(z_{0}\right)$ with center at $z_{0}$ and radius $r_{0}$. For $0<r \leq r_{0}$, let $\sigma_{r}$ the positively oriented circular arc at angle $\alpha$ (Figure 10), consistin $z=z_{0}+r e^{i \theta}$, where $\theta_{0} \leq \theta \leq \theta_{0}+\alpha, \theta_{0}$ and $\alpha$ are fixed, and $\alpha \neq 0$.
(15)
$$
\lim _{r \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{1}{i \alpha} \int_{\sigma_{r}} \frac{f(z)}{z-z_{0}} d z=f\left(z_{0}\right)
$$

Proof Parametrize the integral in (15) using $z=z_{0}+r e^{i \theta}$, where $\theta_{0} \leq \theta \leq d z=r i e^{i \theta} d \theta, z-z_{0}=r e^{i \theta}$. Then
$$
\frac{1}{i \alpha} \int_{\sigma_{r}} \frac{f(z)}{z-z_{0}} d z=\frac{1}{i \alpha} \int_{\theta_{0}}^{\theta_{0}+\alpha} \frac{f\left(z_{0}+r e^{i \theta}\right)}{r e^{i \theta}} i r e^{i \theta} d \theta=\frac{1}{\alpha} \int_{\theta_{0}}^{\theta_{0}+\alpha} f\left(z_{0}+r e^{i \theta}\right.
$$

Let $F(r)=\frac{1}{\alpha} \int_{\theta_{0}}^{\theta_{0}+\alpha} f\left(z_{0}+r e^{i \theta}\right) d \theta$. Since $(r, \theta) \mapsto f\left(z_{0}+r e^{i \theta}\right)$ is continu all $0 \leq r \leq r_{0}$ and all $\theta$, it follows from Theorem 5(i), Section 3.5, that continuous on $\left[0, r_{0}\right]$. Consequently, $\lim _{r \rightarrow 0^{+}} F(r)=F(0)=\frac{1}{\alpha} \int_{\theta_{0}}^{\theta_{0}+\alpha} f(z f\left(z_{0}\right)$, which is equivalent to (15).

The following simple consequence of Lemma 2 is useful.
Suppose that $g(z)$ has a simple pole at $z_{0}$ and for $0<r \leq r_{0}$, let $\sigma_{r}$ the circular arc at angle $\alpha$ (Figure 10). Then
$$
\lim _{r \rightarrow 0^{+}} \int_{\sigma_{r}} g(z) d z=i \alpha \operatorname{Res}\left(g(z), z_{0}\right)
$$

Proof Let $f(z)=\left(z-z_{0}\right) g(z)$ for $z \neq z_{0}$ and define $f\left(z_{0}\right)=\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}}\left(z-z_{0}\right.$ Res $\left(g(z), z_{0}\right)$. Since $g(z)$ has a simple pole at $z_{0}$, it follows from The Section 4.6, that $f(z)$ is analytic at $z_{0}$. Now apply Lemma 2 to $f(z)$ a follows from (15), since for $z \neq z_{0}, f(z) /\left(z-z_{0}\right)=g(z)$.

The improper integrals that we consider next differ from previo in that they are not always convergent. However, their Cauchy p values do exist. Integrals of this sort are important and arise natur example, when computing Hilbert transforms.

EXAMPLE 4 Cauchy principal value: Use of indented contour
Derive the integral identities
$$
\text { P.V. } \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin x}{x-a} d x=\pi \cos a \text { and P.V. } \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\cos x}{x-a} d x=-\pi \sin a, \quad-\infty
$$

In particular, when $a=0$, the first integral yields $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{\sin x}{x} d x=\pi$.
